channel.title: Shiloh Creek
channel.description: Welcome to Shiloh Creek — a quirky nature reserve where Old Timber, a wise old elephant elder, tells stories from the lives of the animals who live there. Bongo the klutzy bear cub, Zippy the hyperactive hummingbird, Shelly the dry-witted tortoise, Garingo the dramatic parrot, Fern the shy fawn, and a chorus of comic frogs called the Mudpuppies all share their adventures, their mistakes, and what they learn from each other. Every story carries a timeless principle from the Bible — never as a lecture, always as part of the story. For kids ages 4-12. Stories of kindness, courage, honesty, patience, and friendship.
channel.itunes:summary: Welcome to Shiloh Creek — a quirky nature reserve where Old Timber, a wise old elephant elder, tells stories from the lives of the animals who live there. Bongo the klutzy bear cub, Zippy the hyperactive hummingbird, Shelly the dry-witted tortoise, Garingo the dramatic parrot, Fern the shy fawn, and a chorus of comic frogs called the Mudpuppies all share their adventures, their mistakes, and what they learn from each other. Every story carries a timeless principle from the Bible — never as a lecture, always as part of the story. For kids ages 4-12. Stories of kindness, courage, honesty, patience, and friendship.
channel.itunes:subtitle: Comedy-driven kid stories with timeless biblical wisdom — told by Old Timber the elephant elder, pondside.
channel.itunes:author: Shiloh Creek

item.title: Welcome to Shiloh Creek
item.itunes:title: Welcome to Shiloh Creek
item.description: Old Timber pulls up by the pond and gathers the friends — and the listener — for the very first story. A shy fawn named Fern arrives in Shiloh Creek for the first time. The animals rush to welcome her — except Shelly the tortoise, who knows that the kindest welcome is sometimes the quietest one. The Bible has a line about loving your neighbour. Fern reminds us how today.
item.itunes:summary: Old Timber pulls up by the pond and gathers the friends — and the listener — for the very first story. A shy fawn named Fern arrives in Shiloh Creek for the first time. The animals rush to welcome her — except Shelly the tortoise, who knows that the kindest welcome is sometimes the quietest one. The Bible has a line about loving your neighbour. Fern reminds us how today.
item.itunes:subtitle: Old Timber pulls up by the pond and gathers the friends — and the listener — for the very first story. A shy fawn named Fern arrives in Shiloh Creek for the first time. The animals rush to welcome her

item.title: The Moonflower
item.itunes:title: The Moonflower
item.description: The night Bongo tried to make the moon hurry up. Fern's first moonflower is about to bloom — but only when the moon decides it's ready. Bongo doesn't want to wait. The Mudpuppies don't want to wait. Even Professor Hoot has theories. Only Fern, quiet by the bud, gets to see the first bloom — and what she learns about being still, the Bible knew first.
item.itunes:summary: The night Bongo tried to make the moon hurry up. Fern's first moonflower is about to bloom — but only when the moon decides it's ready. Bongo doesn't want to wait. The Mudpuppies don't want to wait. Even Professor Hoot has theories. Only Fern, quiet by the bud, gets to see the first bloom — and what she learns about being still, the Bible knew first.
item.itunes:subtitle: The night Bongo tried to make the moon hurry up. Fern's first moonflower is about to bloom — but only when the moon decides it's ready. Bongo doesn't want to wait. The Mudpuppies don't want to wait. E

item.title: The Wind Did It
item.itunes:title: The Wind Did It
item.description: The morning Garingo the parrot blamed the wind for something the wind did not do. Shelly's favourite rock has split in half. Garingo invents the most theatrical lie in the history of Shiloh Creek — a small, local, highly local, undocumented wind. The listener sees the whole thing happen. Garingo learns that the truth needs only one short sentence — and the lie needs many. The Bible has a line about that too.
item.itunes:summary: The morning Garingo the parrot blamed the wind for something the wind did not do. Shelly's favourite rock has split in half. Garingo invents the most theatrical lie in the history of Shiloh Creek — a small, local, highly local, undocumented wind. The listener sees the whole thing happen. Garingo learns that the truth needs only one short sentence — and the lie needs many. The Bible has a line about that too.
item.itunes:subtitle: The morning Garingo the parrot blamed the wind for something the wind did not do. Shelly's favourite rock has split in half. Garingo invents the most theatrical lie in the history of Shiloh Creek — a

item.title: Storm at Shiloh Creek
item.itunes:title: Storm at Shiloh Creek
item.description: A storm rolls into Shiloh Creek and the friends scatter in panic. Bongo wants to fix the sky. Zippy has seven ideas. Garingo announces the wind has feelings. Only Fern — the shyest of them all — quietly remembers the way to a safe lookout. The dear thing who used to say nothing finds she has the words to lead. The Bible has an old line about being brave — and Joshua, who was scared, and led his friends anyway.
item.itunes:summary: A storm rolls into Shiloh Creek and the friends scatter in panic. Bongo wants to fix the sky. Zippy has seven ideas. Garingo announces the wind has feelings. Only Fern — the shyest of them all — quietly remembers the way to a safe lookout. The dear thing who used to say nothing finds she has the words to lead. The Bible has an old line about being brave — and Joshua, who was scared, and led his friends anyway.
item.itunes:subtitle: A storm rolls into Shiloh Creek and the friends scatter in panic. Bongo wants to fix the sky. Zippy has seven ideas. Garingo announces the wind has feelings. Only Fern — the shyest of them all — quiet

item.title: The Great Flood
item.itunes:title: The Great Flood
item.description: The morning Zippy the hummingbird tried to fix the meadow and accidentally made it a lake. The Hydra-Tron Three Thousand was supposed to water everything gently. It did not. Bongo tries to help by digging channels (this makes it worse). Garingo announces the meadow has become a pond. The Mudpuppies are having the best day of their lives. Zippy is devastated. And the friends — slowly, one by one — find their way to forgive her. The Bible has a line about forgiving someone seventy times seven, which is just a way of saying don't keep score.
item.itunes:summary: The morning Zippy the hummingbird tried to fix the meadow and accidentally made it a lake. The Hydra-Tron Three Thousand was supposed to water everything gently. It did not. Bongo tries to help by digging channels (this makes it worse). Garingo announces the meadow has become a pond. The Mudpuppies are having the best day of their lives. Zippy is devastated. And the friends — slowly, one by one — find their way to forgive her. The Bible has a line about forgiving someone seventy times seven, which is just a way of saying don't keep score.
item.itunes:subtitle: The morning Zippy the hummingbird tried to fix the meadow and accidentally made it a lake. The Hydra-Tron Three Thousand was supposed to water everything gently. It did not. Bongo tries to help by dig

item.title: Berry Season
item.itunes:title: Berry Season
item.description: The harvest is small this year. The friends sit at the patch and count — and there aren't enough berries to go around. Zippy does the math. Garingo announces a stress. Shelly arrives with one berry. And Old Timber visits — and reminds the friends, gently, of someone who isn't even at the patch. The Mudpuppies. Jesus once told his friends something about giving — that when you give freely, without counting, the joy comes back full. For kids ages 4-12. Stories of generosity from the lives of Old Timber and the friends at Shiloh Creek.
item.itunes:summary: The harvest is small this year. The friends sit at the patch and count — and there aren't enough berries to go around. Zippy does the math. Garingo announces a stress. Shelly arrives with one berry. And Old Timber visits — and reminds the friends, gently, of someone who isn't even at the patch. The Mudpuppies. Jesus once told his friends something about giving — that when you give freely, without counting, the joy comes back full. For kids ages 4-12. Stories of generosity from the lives of Old Timber and the friends at Shiloh Creek.
item.itunes:subtitle: The harvest is small this year. The friends sit at the patch and count — and there aren't enough berries to go around. Zippy does the math. Garingo announces a stress. Shelly arrives with one berry. A

item.title: The Path Through
item.itunes:title: The Path Through
item.description: Bongo the bear cub has a plan — and a leaf-map. He's leading his friends to the old barn for the late-summer gathering. But a storm in the night has shifted a big branch across the path, and the way Bongo drew on his leaf-map isn't there anymore. King Solomon once wrote in the Bible — trust in the Lord, lean not unto thine own understanding. Bongo learns what it means to stop pretending to know the way, and let the Lord show him a path he could never have drawn himself. For kids ages 4-12. Stories of trust and quiet wisdom from Old Timber and the friends at Shiloh Creek.
item.itunes:summary: Bongo the bear cub has a plan — and a leaf-map. He's leading his friends to the old barn for the late-summer gathering. But a storm in the night has shifted a big branch across the path, and the way Bongo drew on his leaf-map isn't there anymore. King Solomon once wrote in the Bible — trust in the Lord, lean not unto thine own understanding. Bongo learns what it means to stop pretending to know the way, and let the Lord show him a path he could never have drawn himself. For kids ages 4-12. Stories of trust and quiet wisdom from Old Timber and the friends at Shiloh Creek.
item.itunes:subtitle: Bongo the bear cub has a plan — and a leaf-map. He's leading his friends to the old barn for the late-summer gathering. But a storm in the night has shifted a big branch across the path, and the way B

item.title: Best Rainy Day Ever
item.itunes:title: Best Rainy Day Ever
item.description: Shelly leads a rainy-day adventure when the planned swim is cancelled. Worms emerge, a frog sings under a leaf, leaves drink. The friends learn what it means to give thanks in the day you got — not the day you wanted. Anchored in the Apostle Paul's letter to the Thessalonians — in every thing give thanks. For kids ages 4-12. Stories of gratitude and quiet noticing from Old Timber and the friends at Shiloh Creek.
item.itunes:summary: Shelly leads a rainy-day adventure when the planned swim is cancelled. Worms emerge, a frog sings under a leaf, leaves drink. The friends learn what it means to give thanks in the day you got — not the day you wanted. Anchored in the Apostle Paul's letter to the Thessalonians — in every thing give thanks. For kids ages 4-12. Stories of gratitude and quiet noticing from Old Timber and the friends at Shiloh Creek.
item.itunes:subtitle: Shelly leads a rainy-day adventure when the planned swim is cancelled. Worms emerge, a frog sings under a leaf, leaves drink. The friends learn what it means to give thanks in the day you got — not th

item.title: The Bridge
item.itunes:title: The Bridge
item.description: Bongo the bear cub is sure he can lift the fallen log across the path by himself. He's strong. He's a bear. Bears lift logs. But the log won't move — and his friends are waiting. Anchored in Ecclesiastes chapter four, verse nine — two are better than one. King Solomon's old wisdom lived out in a bear cub learning to ask for help BEFORE he lifts. For kids ages 4-12. Stories of teamwork and quiet wisdom from Old Timber and the friends at Shiloh Creek.
item.itunes:summary: Bongo the bear cub is sure he can lift the fallen log across the path by himself. He's strong. He's a bear. Bears lift logs. But the log won't move — and his friends are waiting. Anchored in Ecclesiastes chapter four, verse nine — two are better than one. King Solomon's old wisdom lived out in a bear cub learning to ask for help BEFORE he lifts. For kids ages 4-12. Stories of teamwork and quiet wisdom from Old Timber and the friends at Shiloh Creek.
item.itunes:subtitle: Bongo the bear cub is sure he can lift the fallen log across the path by himself. He's strong. He's a bear. Bears lift logs. But the log won't move — and his friends are waiting. Anchored in Ecclesias

item.title: The Visitor
item.itunes:title: The Visitor
item.description: The story of the morning a small fast-chittering chipmunk showed up at the meadow with what sounded like a very grievous complaint — and how Garingo the parrot tried something he had never tried before. A soft answer. Anchored in Proverbs chapter fifteen, verse one — a soft answer turneth away wrath: but grievous words stir up anger. King Solomon's old wisdom lived out in a theatrical parrot learning that the loud thing in his throat is not always the truest one. For kids ages 4-12. Stories of self-control and de-escalation from Old Timber and the friends at Shiloh Creek.
item.itunes:summary: The story of the morning a small fast-chittering chipmunk showed up at the meadow with what sounded like a very grievous complaint — and how Garingo the parrot tried something he had never tried before. A soft answer. Anchored in Proverbs chapter fifteen, verse one — a soft answer turneth away wrath: but grievous words stir up anger. King Solomon's old wisdom lived out in a theatrical parrot learning that the loud thing in his throat is not always the truest one. For kids ages 4-12. Stories of self-control and de-escalation from Old Timber and the friends at Shiloh Creek.
item.itunes:subtitle: The story of the morning a small fast-chittering chipmunk showed up at the meadow with what sounded like a very grievous complaint — and how Garingo the parrot tried something he had never tried befor

item.title: Shiloh Creek Olympics
item.itunes:title: Shiloh Creek Olympics
item.description: The morning Shelly the tortoise crossed her first threshold — saying yes to her first race. Bongo bounds. Zippy zips. Garingo flies low and theatrical. Shelly walks. Slowly. Each step is HER race — against the part of her that wants to quit. And when the meadow goes quiet from misplaced kindness, the verse Old Timber whispered to her at dawn comes back, in her own voice, and she keeps walking. Anchored in Paul's letter to the Hebrews, chapter twelve, verse one — the cloud of witnesses and the race that is set before us. For kids ages 4-12. Stories of perseverance and the quiet courage of starting from Old Timber and the friends at Shiloh Creek.
item.itunes:summary: The morning Shelly the tortoise crossed her first threshold — saying yes to her first race. Bongo bounds. Zippy zips. Garingo flies low and theatrical. Shelly walks. Slowly. Each step is HER race — against the part of her that wants to quit. And when the meadow goes quiet from misplaced kindness, the verse Old Timber whispered to her at dawn comes back, in her own voice, and she keeps walking. Anchored in Paul's letter to the Hebrews, chapter twelve, verse one — the cloud of witnesses and the race that is set before us. For kids ages 4-12. Stories of perseverance and the quiet courage of starting from Old Timber and the friends at Shiloh Creek.
item.itunes:subtitle: The morning Shelly the tortoise crossed her first threshold — saying yes to her first race. Bongo bounds. Zippy zips. Garingo flies low and theatrical. Shelly walks. Slowly. Each step is HER race — ag

item.title: The Surprise
item.itunes:title: The Surprise
item.description: Season 1 finale. The friends plan a quiet surprise for Ranger Ray, who has been gone on his ranger circuit. Fern leads — her seed-of-anxious-debut now arc-paid-off as confident orchestrator. Each prep-detail weaves a memory from a prior episode: lanterns from a flood, berries from a recovered patch, the hidden path from a storm, Shelly's slow procession, Chitter returning welcome. When Ranger Ray arrives, he names the small kind things he saw all season — love that remembers, love that doesn't parade. Anchored in Paul's letter to the Corinthians, chapter thirteen, verse four — charity suffereth long, and is kind. For kids ages 4-12. The Season 1 closing story from Old Timber and the friends at Shiloh Creek.
item.itunes:summary: Season 1 finale. The friends plan a quiet surprise for Ranger Ray, who has been gone on his ranger circuit. Fern leads — her seed-of-anxious-debut now arc-paid-off as confident orchestrator. Each prep-detail weaves a memory from a prior episode: lanterns from a flood, berries from a recovered patch, the hidden path from a storm, Shelly's slow procession, Chitter returning welcome. When Ranger Ray arrives, he names the small kind things he saw all season — love that remembers, love that doesn't parade. Anchored in Paul's letter to the Corinthians, chapter thirteen, verse four — charity suffereth long, and is kind. For kids ages 4-12. The Season 1 closing story from Old Timber and the friends at Shiloh Creek.
item.itunes:subtitle: Season 1 finale. The friends plan a quiet surprise for Ranger Ray, who has been gone on his ranger circuit. Fern leads — her seed-of-anxious-debut now arc-paid-off as confident orchestrator. Each prep

item.title: The Smartest Animal Contest
item.itunes:title: The Smartest Animal Contest
item.description: Season 2 begins. Professor Hoot announces a contest to settle, once and for all, who is the smartest animal in Shiloh Creek — and fully expects to win it himself. But the day the quietest, smallest friend helps without being asked, Hoot learns that being the smartest isn't the same as being right. The last become first. Anchored in a line Jesus told his friends — the last shall be first, and the first last. For kids ages 4-12. A new season of stories from Old Timber and the friends at Shiloh Creek.
item.itunes:summary: Season 2 begins. Professor Hoot announces a contest to settle, once and for all, who is the smartest animal in Shiloh Creek — and fully expects to win it himself. But the day the quietest, smallest friend helps without being asked, Hoot learns that being the smartest isn't the same as being right. The last become first. Anchored in a line Jesus told his friends — the last shall be first, and the first last. For kids ages 4-12. A new season of stories from Old Timber and the friends at Shiloh Creek.
item.itunes:subtitle: Season 2 begins. Professor Hoot announces a contest to settle, once and for all, who is the smartest animal in Shiloh Creek — and fully expects to win it himself. But the day the quietest, smallest fr

item.title: The Old Grudge
item.itunes:title: The Old Grudge
item.description: An old falling-out between Garingo the parrot and Shelly the tortoise — quiet for a long time — finally surfaces. The friends already learned to forgive once. But what about the SAME hurt, coming back again? With a little help from Old Timber and a fact about penguins who take turns at the cold edge of the huddle, Garingo and Shelly learn that forgiveness isn't one big moment — it's a thing you practise, and it's allowed to be hard the second time. Paul once wrote to his friends in Colossae: bear with one another, and forgive. For kids ages 4-12.
item.itunes:summary: An old falling-out between Garingo the parrot and Shelly the tortoise — quiet for a long time — finally surfaces. The friends already learned to forgive once. But what about the SAME hurt, coming back again? With a little help from Old Timber and a fact about penguins who take turns at the cold edge of the huddle, Garingo and Shelly learn that forgiveness isn't one big moment — it's a thing you practise, and it's allowed to be hard the second time. Paul once wrote to his friends in Colossae: bear with one another, and forgive. For kids ages 4-12.
item.itunes:subtitle: An old falling-out between Garingo the parrot and Shelly the tortoise — quiet for a long time — finally surfaces. The friends already learned to forgive once. But what about the SAME hurt, coming back

item.title: The Long Friend
item.itunes:title: The Long Friend
item.description: Old Timber needs the spare lantern from the big old barn before the light goes — and the only two friends at the pond are Bongo, who is all hurry, and Shelly, who is all wait. Zippy, Fern, and Garingo wave the mismatched pair off; then it's just the quick one and the slow one, together, on a small quest down the winding path. About a bear cub who keeps charging ahead and a tortoise who keeps being right about the slow way round; about a mud-slick where Bongo's speed is exactly wrong and Shelly's care is exactly right, and a fallen reed-gate where Shelly's pace is exactly wrong and Bongo's strong arms are exactly right; about the plain hurt of feeling too-slow and too-much, said out loud at last; about great grey elephants who remember an old friend across many, many years and call out a special hello when they meet again. The friends come home, the others welcome them back, and Old Timber asks the two of them what they learned — then lands the lesson, and at the very end shares the line a wise king named Solomon wrote down about a friend who loves at all times. With Professor Hoot's Totally True Facts on the V-formation of geese — the most well-mannered traffic in the sky — and a Mudpuppy Minutes that gets friendship gloriously, confidently wrong.
item.itunes:summary: Old Timber needs the spare lantern from the big old barn before the light goes — and the only two friends at the pond are Bongo, who is all hurry, and Shelly, who is all wait. Zippy, Fern, and Garingo wave the mismatched pair off; then it's just the quick one and the slow one, together, on a small quest down the winding path. About a bear cub who keeps charging ahead and a tortoise who keeps being right about the slow way round; about a mud-slick where Bongo's speed is exactly wrong and Shelly's care is exactly right, and a fallen reed-gate where Shelly's pace is exactly wrong and Bongo's strong arms are exactly right; about the plain hurt of feeling too-slow and too-much, said out loud at last; about great grey elephants who remember an old friend across many, many years and call out a special hello when they meet again. The friends come home, the others welcome them back, and Old Timber asks the two of them what they learned — then lands the lesson, and at the very end shares the line a wise king named Solomon wrote down about a friend who loves at all times. With Professor Hoot's Totally True Facts on the V-formation of geese — the most well-mannered traffic in the sky — and a Mudpuppy Minutes that gets friendship gloriously, confidently wrong.
item.itunes:subtitle: Old Timber needs the spare lantern from the big old barn before the light goes — and the only two friends at the pond are Bongo, who is all hurry, and Shelly, who is all wait. Zippy, Fern, and Garingo

item.title: The New One
item.itunes:title: The New One
item.description: A traveller from the high dry country comes to the creek with a quiet, unhurried way of saying hello — and fast little Zippy zips right past her. But when the friends climb up to the cold thin air of the high country, it is Zippy who is suddenly the new one, rushed-past and left behind — and she learns, from the inside, what she did that morning. A visitor-welcome story about the Golden Rule, lived. Anchored in a line a teacher named Jesus taught, written down in the Bible — and as ye would that men should do to you, do ye also to them likewise. For kids ages 4-12. Stories of kindness and empathy from Old Timber and the friends at Shiloh Creek.
item.itunes:summary: A traveller from the high dry country comes to the creek with a quiet, unhurried way of saying hello — and fast little Zippy zips right past her. But when the friends climb up to the cold thin air of the high country, it is Zippy who is suddenly the new one, rushed-past and left behind — and she learns, from the inside, what she did that morning. A visitor-welcome story about the Golden Rule, lived. Anchored in a line a teacher named Jesus taught, written down in the Bible — and as ye would that men should do to you, do ye also to them likewise. For kids ages 4-12. Stories of kindness and empathy from Old Timber and the friends at Shiloh Creek.
item.itunes:subtitle: A traveller from the high dry country comes to the creek with a quiet, unhurried way of saying hello — and fast little Zippy zips right past her. But when the friends climb up to the cold thin air of

item.title: The Little Voice
item.itunes:title: The Little Voice
item.description: Fern hears something nobody else does — a tiny cricket, hidden under the very reeds the friends are about to clear for a big building project. The cricket can't talk; it can only chirp. Fern noticed it, but she is too shy to speak up while Bongo, Garingo, and Zippy boom ahead with the plan. As the work rushes toward the cricket's hiding place, Fern has to choose: stay quiet and safe, or open her mouth for the one small voice that can't speak for itself. A first-solo-lead courage-of-conscience story about being an upstander, not a bystander — using your voice for someone who can't use theirs. Anchored in a line a mother once taught her son, who grew up to be a king, written down in the Bible to this day: open your mouth for the mute, in the cause of all who are left desolate. With Professor Hoot's Totally True Facts on the meerkat sentinel — the one small lookout who calls out for the whole family — and a Mudpuppy Minutes that explains, gloriously and confidently wrong, why crickets chirp at night. For kids ages 4-12. Stories of courage and kindness from Old Timber and the friends at Shiloh Creek.
item.itunes:summary: Fern hears something nobody else does — a tiny cricket, hidden under the very reeds the friends are about to clear for a big building project. The cricket can't talk; it can only chirp. Fern noticed it, but she is too shy to speak up while Bongo, Garingo, and Zippy boom ahead with the plan. As the work rushes toward the cricket's hiding place, Fern has to choose: stay quiet and safe, or open her mouth for the one small voice that can't speak for itself. A first-solo-lead courage-of-conscience story about being an upstander, not a bystander — using your voice for someone who can't use theirs. Anchored in a line a mother once taught her son, who grew up to be a king, written down in the Bible to this day: open your mouth for the mute, in the cause of all who are left desolate. With Professor Hoot's Totally True Facts on the meerkat sentinel — the one small lookout who calls out for the whole family — and a Mudpuppy Minutes that explains, gloriously and confidently wrong, why crickets chirp at night. For kids ages 4-12. Stories of courage and kindness from Old Timber and the friends at Shiloh Creek.
item.itunes:subtitle: Fern hears something nobody else does — a tiny cricket, hidden under the very reeds the friends are about to clear for a big building project. The cricket can't talk; it can only chirp. Fern noticed i

item.title: The Glad Goodbye
item.itunes:title: The Glad Goodbye
item.description: It's Fern's last morning at the creek — she's grown brave enough to go explore the wide water downstream, and she's happy to go. But the friends droop into a grey, no-one-wants-to-play gloom. Then a brand-new otter named Rex slides in, joyful and certain he can fix sad with backflips — and learns, the hard way, that you can't cheer a sadness away. A goodbye-and-hello story about choosing a cheerful heart that doesn't pretend the hard away: being glad FOR a friend and sad to see them go, both at once, out loud, together. Anchored in a line the wise King Solomon wrote down in the Bible, in the book of Proverbs — a cheerful heart makes good medicine. With Professor Hoot's Totally True Facts on why river otters slide for the pure joy of it, and a Mudpuppy Minutes that explains, confidently and gloriously wrong, where a creek goes. For kids ages 4-12. Stories of kindness and courage from Old Timber and the friends at Shiloh Creek.
item.itunes:summary: It's Fern's last morning at the creek — she's grown brave enough to go explore the wide water downstream, and she's happy to go. But the friends droop into a grey, no-one-wants-to-play gloom. Then a brand-new otter named Rex slides in, joyful and certain he can fix sad with backflips — and learns, the hard way, that you can't cheer a sadness away. A goodbye-and-hello story about choosing a cheerful heart that doesn't pretend the hard away: being glad FOR a friend and sad to see them go, both at once, out loud, together. Anchored in a line the wise King Solomon wrote down in the Bible, in the book of Proverbs — a cheerful heart makes good medicine. With Professor Hoot's Totally True Facts on why river otters slide for the pure joy of it, and a Mudpuppy Minutes that explains, confidently and gloriously wrong, where a creek goes. For kids ages 4-12. Stories of kindness and courage from Old Timber and the friends at Shiloh Creek.
item.itunes:subtitle: It's Fern's last morning at the creek — she's grown brave enough to go explore the wide water downstream, and she's happy to go. But the friends droop into a grey, no-one-wants-to-play gloom. Then a b

item.title: The Not-Yet Day
item.itunes:title: The Not-Yet Day
item.description: Zippy the hummingbird CANNOT wait. There's a chrysalis on a branch about to become a butterfly — "almost ready," everyone keeps saying — and almost-ready is the hardest thing Zippy has ever heard. She tries fanning it, singing to it, even helping it open early... and nearly spoils the very thing she's been longing for. With Rex right beside her, the friends learn to wait together — and the butterfly comes out, in its own good time, all the bigger for the waiting. A gentle story about patience: that good things keep their own season, and the waiting can be part of the gift — even waiting for a friend to come home (the friends miss Fern, away on her adventure). Anchored in a line the wise King Solomon wrote down in the Bible, in the book of Ecclesiastes — to every thing there is a season. With Professor Hoot's Totally True Facts on the cicadas that wait underground for years, and a Mudpuppy Minutes that explains, confidently and gloriously wrong, why morning always comes. For kids ages 4-12. Stories of patience and kindness from Old Timber and the friends at Shiloh Creek.
item.itunes:summary: Zippy the hummingbird CANNOT wait. There's a chrysalis on a branch about to become a butterfly — "almost ready," everyone keeps saying — and almost-ready is the hardest thing Zippy has ever heard. She tries fanning it, singing to it, even helping it open early... and nearly spoils the very thing she's been longing for. With Rex right beside her, the friends learn to wait together — and the butterfly comes out, in its own good time, all the bigger for the waiting. A gentle story about patience: that good things keep their own season, and the waiting can be part of the gift — even waiting for a friend to come home (the friends miss Fern, away on her adventure). Anchored in a line the wise King Solomon wrote down in the Bible, in the book of Ecclesiastes — to every thing there is a season. With Professor Hoot's Totally True Facts on the cicadas that wait underground for years, and a Mudpuppy Minutes that explains, confidently and gloriously wrong, why morning always comes. For kids ages 4-12. Stories of patience and kindness from Old Timber and the friends at Shiloh Creek.
item.itunes:subtitle: Zippy the hummingbird CANNOT wait. There's a chrysalis on a branch about to become a butterfly — "almost ready," everyone keeps saying — and almost-ready is the hardest thing Zippy has ever heard. She

item.title: The Brave Small Thing
item.itunes:title: The Brave Small Thing
item.description: The Mudpuppies — Splish, Splash, and Splosh — have never led an adventure before. So when a kind little errand sends the three tiny frogs out of their cozy pond all the way to the big old barn at dusk, they do what small-and-scared folks often do: they puff up LOUD and pretend they're not frightened at all ("we're barn experts — we've never been inside, which is HOW we know so much!"). But bravado runs out at the dark doorway, and Splish finally says the true thing: "I've been scared the whole time." Together — naming the fear, trusting God is right there with them — they do the brave thing anyway, and find the dark was warm and kind all along. A gentle story about real courage: not pretending you're not scared, but saying so and going anyway, with a friend. Anchored in a line a young shepherd named David — who became a king — wrote in the Bible when he was truly afraid, in the book of Psalms: what time I am afraid, I will trust in thee. With Professor Hoot's Totally True Facts on the tiny Arctic tern that flies the longest journey on Earth. For kids ages 4-12. Stories of courage and kindness from Old Timber and the friends at Shiloh Creek.
item.itunes:summary: The Mudpuppies — Splish, Splash, and Splosh — have never led an adventure before. So when a kind little errand sends the three tiny frogs out of their cozy pond all the way to the big old barn at dusk, they do what small-and-scared folks often do: they puff up LOUD and pretend they're not frightened at all ("we're barn experts — we've never been inside, which is HOW we know so much!"). But bravado runs out at the dark doorway, and Splish finally says the true thing: "I've been scared the whole time." Together — naming the fear, trusting God is right there with them — they do the brave thing anyway, and find the dark was warm and kind all along. A gentle story about real courage: not pretending you're not scared, but saying so and going anyway, with a friend. Anchored in a line a young shepherd named David — who became a king — wrote in the Bible when he was truly afraid, in the book of Psalms: what time I am afraid, I will trust in thee. With Professor Hoot's Totally True Facts on the tiny Arctic tern that flies the longest journey on Earth. For kids ages 4-12. Stories of courage and kindness from Old Timber and the friends at Shiloh Creek.
item.itunes:subtitle: The Mudpuppies — Splish, Splash, and Splosh — have never led an adventure before. So when a kind little errand sends the three tiny frogs out of their cozy pond all the way to the big old barn at dusk

item.title: The Very Good Day
item.itunes:title: The Very Good Day
item.description: Something happens at Shiloh Creek that has never happened before: a human comes to visit — a curious, kind, clever boy named Jigstar, who follows the water right into the middle of the morning. The animals are amazed ("Where's your TAIL? Can you do a BACKFLIP? Can you SLIDE?"), but then Jigstar turns the question around and asks THEM: do you ever wish you were something else? One by one the friends admit a secret wish — fast Zippy wishes she could be still, slow Shelly wishes she were quick, and loud Garingo wishes he were as brave on the inside as he sounds on the outside — until it starts to seem like nobody is glad to be themselves. It takes the boy's fresh eyes to help the whole Creek see what they couldn't: each one was made different, on purpose, and that is exactly the good of it. A warm story about belonging and being made wonderful — that you don't have to be like anyone else to be made just right. Anchored in a line from the very first pages of the Bible, in the book of Genesis: God saw everything that he had made, and behold, it was very good. With Professor Hoot's Totally True Facts on the platypus — the animal that looks like it's made of bits of every other animal, and is wholly, perfectly itself — and a Mudpuppy Minutes that explains, confidently and gloriously wrong, what a human is. For kids ages 4-12. Stories of kindness and wonder from Old Timber and the friends at Shiloh Creek.
item.itunes:summary: Something happens at Shiloh Creek that has never happened before: a human comes to visit — a curious, kind, clever boy named Jigstar, who follows the water right into the middle of the morning. The animals are amazed ("Where's your TAIL? Can you do a BACKFLIP? Can you SLIDE?"), but then Jigstar turns the question around and asks THEM: do you ever wish you were something else? One by one the friends admit a secret wish — fast Zippy wishes she could be still, slow Shelly wishes she were quick, and loud Garingo wishes he were as brave on the inside as he sounds on the outside — until it starts to seem like nobody is glad to be themselves. It takes the boy's fresh eyes to help the whole Creek see what they couldn't: each one was made different, on purpose, and that is exactly the good of it. A warm story about belonging and being made wonderful — that you don't have to be like anyone else to be made just right. Anchored in a line from the very first pages of the Bible, in the book of Genesis: God saw everything that he had made, and behold, it was very good. With Professor Hoot's Totally True Facts on the platypus — the animal that looks like it's made of bits of every other animal, and is wholly, perfectly itself — and a Mudpuppy Minutes that explains, confidently and gloriously wrong, what a human is. For kids ages 4-12. Stories of kindness and wonder from Old Timber and the friends at Shiloh Creek.
item.itunes:subtitle: Something happens at Shiloh Creek that has never happened before: a human comes to visit — a curious, kind, clever boy named Jigstar, who follows the water right into the middle of the morning. The an

item.title: The Mending Day
item.itunes:title: The Mending Day
item.description: Bongo the bear cub LOVES to help — he's just a bit of a klutz about it. So when he tries to tidy up for Old Timber and accidentally knocks the elephant's most precious keepsake — a smooth memory-stone he's carried since he was young — and it cracks clean in two, Bongo does the thing every kid has been tempted to do: he hides the pieces and hopes nobody finds out. But the hiding turns out to be far heavier to carry than the truth. With a gentle nudge from Shelly ("heavy things get lighter when you set them down"), Bongo finally owns up — and Old Timber meets him not with anger but with mercy, and together they mend the stone, the new seam becoming Old Timber's favorite mark of all. A warm story about honesty and grace: that covering up a mistake keeps it heavy, but telling the truth brings mercy. Anchored in a line the wise King Solomon wrote in the Bible, in the book of Proverbs: whoever conceals their wrongs won't prosper, but whoever confesses them finds mercy. With Professor Hoot's Totally True Facts on how trees grow around their wounds — the mark staying as part of the story — and a Mudpuppy Minutes that explains, confidently and gloriously wrong, how broken things get mended. For kids ages 4-12. Stories of honesty and kindness from Old Timber and the friends at Shiloh Creek.
item.itunes:summary: Bongo the bear cub LOVES to help — he's just a bit of a klutz about it. So when he tries to tidy up for Old Timber and accidentally knocks the elephant's most precious keepsake — a smooth memory-stone he's carried since he was young — and it cracks clean in two, Bongo does the thing every kid has been tempted to do: he hides the pieces and hopes nobody finds out. But the hiding turns out to be far heavier to carry than the truth. With a gentle nudge from Shelly ("heavy things get lighter when you set them down"), Bongo finally owns up — and Old Timber meets him not with anger but with mercy, and together they mend the stone, the new seam becoming Old Timber's favorite mark of all. A warm story about honesty and grace: that covering up a mistake keeps it heavy, but telling the truth brings mercy. Anchored in a line the wise King Solomon wrote in the Bible, in the book of Proverbs: whoever conceals their wrongs won't prosper, but whoever confesses them finds mercy. With Professor Hoot's Totally True Facts on how trees grow around their wounds — the mark staying as part of the story — and a Mudpuppy Minutes that explains, confidently and gloriously wrong, how broken things get mended. For kids ages 4-12. Stories of honesty and kindness from Old Timber and the friends at Shiloh Creek.
item.itunes:subtitle: Bongo the bear cub LOVES to help — he's just a bit of a klutz about it. So when he tries to tidy up for Old Timber and accidentally knocks the elephant's most precious keepsake — a smooth memory-stone

item.title: The Quiet Ears
item.itunes:title: The Quiet Ears
item.description: A traveling storyteller hedgehog named Bramble wanders into Shiloh Creek the very morning Chitter the chipmunk races back with big news: the bend-tree's first blossom has opened — and it only stays open for one morning! But Chitter talks SO fast, and everyone's only half-listening, that his little message gets passed friend-to-friend and comes out the other end wonderfully wrong — a blossom TOSS, then a blossom CONTEST, then a whole blossom PARTY (bring snacks!). While the loud friends build a party at the wrong tree, quiet Quilla — who heard it right the first time — can't get a word in. It takes Bramble's gentle story about a creature with ears so big it could hear a pinecone drop a mile off, yet never heard the small friend whispering right beside it, to help the friends finally get quiet, really listen, and catch the little blossom just before it tucks itself away. A warm, funny story about how listening isn't hearing everything — it's getting quiet enough to hear the one right in front of you. Anchored in a line from a letter a man named James wrote to his friends in the Bible: be swift to hear, slow to speak. With a Mudpuppy Minutes that explains, gloriously and confidently wrong, what sound is even made of. For kids ages 4-12. Stories of kindness and wonder from Old Timber and the friends at Shiloh Creek.
item.itunes:summary: A traveling storyteller hedgehog named Bramble wanders into Shiloh Creek the very morning Chitter the chipmunk races back with big news: the bend-tree's first blossom has opened — and it only stays open for one morning! But Chitter talks SO fast, and everyone's only half-listening, that his little message gets passed friend-to-friend and comes out the other end wonderfully wrong — a blossom TOSS, then a blossom CONTEST, then a whole blossom PARTY (bring snacks!). While the loud friends build a party at the wrong tree, quiet Quilla — who heard it right the first time — can't get a word in. It takes Bramble's gentle story about a creature with ears so big it could hear a pinecone drop a mile off, yet never heard the small friend whispering right beside it, to help the friends finally get quiet, really listen, and catch the little blossom just before it tucks itself away. A warm, funny story about how listening isn't hearing everything — it's getting quiet enough to hear the one right in front of you. Anchored in a line from a letter a man named James wrote to his friends in the Bible: be swift to hear, slow to speak. With a Mudpuppy Minutes that explains, gloriously and confidently wrong, what sound is even made of. For kids ages 4-12. Stories of kindness and wonder from Old Timber and the friends at Shiloh Creek.
item.itunes:subtitle: A traveling storyteller hedgehog named Bramble wanders into Shiloh Creek the very morning Chitter the chipmunk races back with big news: the bend-tree's first blossom has opened — and it only stays op

item.title: Guess Who's Back
item.itunes:title: Guess Who's Back
item.description: The Season 2 finale! It's a golden evening at Shiloh Creek when a familiar shape steps out of the tall grass — Fern the fawn is HOME, back from the big adventure she set off on, all the way over the ridge to the wide water. And she's not alone: she's brought a human boy she met on the road. But when the friends all shout his name, it's Fern who freezes — because the kind boy who walked her home turns out to be Jigstar, the Creek's very own guest-friend, who'd heard them miss her and recognized her from her own stories. As the friends gather by the water, Fern tells where she went and what she found — and the little creek turns out to be connected, running all the way down to join the wide water. A warm, funny homecoming about the truest treasure of any adventure: the friends you carry in your heart and come home to. Anchored in a line a man named Paul wrote to his friends in the Bible, in his letter to the Philippians: 'I thank my God upon every remembrance of you.' With a wide-water wonder, a Mudpuppy Minutes about where water goes, and Jigstar heading home to the human world (door open for next time). For kids ages 4-12. Stories of kindness and wonder from Old Timber and the friends at Shiloh Creek.
item.itunes:summary: The Season 2 finale! It's a golden evening at Shiloh Creek when a familiar shape steps out of the tall grass — Fern the fawn is HOME, back from the big adventure she set off on, all the way over the ridge to the wide water. And she's not alone: she's brought a human boy she met on the road. But when the friends all shout his name, it's Fern who freezes — because the kind boy who walked her home turns out to be Jigstar, the Creek's very own guest-friend, who'd heard them miss her and recognized her from her own stories. As the friends gather by the water, Fern tells where she went and what she found — and the little creek turns out to be connected, running all the way down to join the wide water. A warm, funny homecoming about the truest treasure of any adventure: the friends you carry in your heart and come home to. Anchored in a line a man named Paul wrote to his friends in the Bible, in his letter to the Philippians: 'I thank my God upon every remembrance of you.' With a wide-water wonder, a Mudpuppy Minutes about where water goes, and Jigstar heading home to the human world (door open for next time). For kids ages 4-12. Stories of kindness and wonder from Old Timber and the friends at Shiloh Creek.
item.itunes:subtitle: The Season 2 finale! It's a golden evening at Shiloh Creek when a familiar shape steps out of the tall grass — Fern the fawn is HOME, back from the big adventure she set off on, all the way over the r

item.title: The Morning That Started Twice
item.itunes:title: The Morning That Started Twice
item.description: The Season 3 premiere — welcome back to Shiloh Creek, and it's a brand-new season AND a brand-new morning! When Bongo's morning goes all wrong — spilled berries, a tipped dew-bucket, and a wake-up song that comes out like a duck stepping on a kazoo — he decides the whole day is RUINED and tries to skip straight to tomorrow. But Old Timber and the friends show him something wonderful: a morning gets to start over, and yesterday's mess-up doesn't have to weigh down today. A warm, funny story about fresh starts, anchored in a line from the Bible, in a book called Lamentations: God's kindness is 'new every morning.' With a Professor Hoot fact about the dawn chorus, a Mudpuppy Minutes about what makes morning happen, and Fern home and glad to be back. For kids ages 4-12. Stories of kindness and wonder from Old Timber and the friends at Shiloh Creek.
item.itunes:summary: The Season 3 premiere — welcome back to Shiloh Creek, and it's a brand-new season AND a brand-new morning! When Bongo's morning goes all wrong — spilled berries, a tipped dew-bucket, and a wake-up song that comes out like a duck stepping on a kazoo — he decides the whole day is RUINED and tries to skip straight to tomorrow. But Old Timber and the friends show him something wonderful: a morning gets to start over, and yesterday's mess-up doesn't have to weigh down today. A warm, funny story about fresh starts, anchored in a line from the Bible, in a book called Lamentations: God's kindness is 'new every morning.' With a Professor Hoot fact about the dawn chorus, a Mudpuppy Minutes about what makes morning happen, and Fern home and glad to be back. For kids ages 4-12. Stories of kindness and wonder from Old Timber and the friends at Shiloh Creek.
item.itunes:subtitle: The Season 3 premiere — welcome back to Shiloh Creek, and it's a brand-new season AND a brand-new morning! When Bongo's morning goes all wrong — spilled berries, a tipped dew-bucket, and a wake-up son

item.title: Old Timber's Turn
item.itunes:title: Old Timber's Turn
item.description: Bramble the traveling storyteller is back at Shiloh Creek with one of his little tales — a story about two animals who each built half of an old stone wall, and the morning a young one asked which half was the good half. Old Timber, delighted, knows at once what it means... or thinks he does. But when little Zippy hears something Old Timber breezed right past — the part where the builders only finished the wall when they helped each other in the middle — the wisest, oldest one at the creek goes quiet, and then he smiles: an old grey head, learning a new thing. A warm, funny story about staying teachable — that even the wise keep learning, and you don't have to know everything. Anchored in a line from the Bible, from the book of Micah: do justly, love mercy, and walk humbly. With a Professor Hoot fact about creatures that keep learning their whole lives, and a Mudpuppy Minutes from three frogs gloriously certain they already know it all. For kids ages 4-12. Stories of kindness and wonder from Old Timber and the friends at Shiloh Creek.
item.itunes:summary: Bramble the traveling storyteller is back at Shiloh Creek with one of his little tales — a story about two animals who each built half of an old stone wall, and the morning a young one asked which half was the good half. Old Timber, delighted, knows at once what it means... or thinks he does. But when little Zippy hears something Old Timber breezed right past — the part where the builders only finished the wall when they helped each other in the middle — the wisest, oldest one at the creek goes quiet, and then he smiles: an old grey head, learning a new thing. A warm, funny story about staying teachable — that even the wise keep learning, and you don't have to know everything. Anchored in a line from the Bible, from the book of Micah: do justly, love mercy, and walk humbly. With a Professor Hoot fact about creatures that keep learning their whole lives, and a Mudpuppy Minutes from three frogs gloriously certain they already know it all. For kids ages 4-12. Stories of kindness and wonder from Old Timber and the friends at Shiloh Creek.
item.itunes:subtitle: Bramble the traveling storyteller is back at Shiloh Creek with one of his little tales — a story about two animals who each built half of an old stone wall, and the morning a young one asked which hal

item.title: The Show Nobody Saw
item.itunes:title: The Show Nobody Saw
item.description: Garingo prepares the grandest act of his life for Show-and-Tell Morning — then finds a friend's spilled seed-jars with nobody watching, and quietly gives up his whole show to set them right. A warm story about kindness that counts even when nobody sees it: the part of you that matters most is the part only God sees. Bible verse: 1 Samuel 16:7.
item.itunes:summary: Garingo prepares the grandest act of his life for Show-and-Tell Morning — then finds a friend's spilled seed-jars with nobody watching, and quietly gives up his whole show to set them right. A warm story about kindness that counts even when nobody sees it: the part of you that matters most is the part only God sees. Bible verse: 1 Samuel 16:7.
item.itunes:subtitle: Garingo prepares the grandest act of his life for Show-and-Tell Morning — then finds a friend's spilled seed-jars with nobody watching, and quietly gives up his whole show to set them right. A warm st
item.itunes:keywords: kids podcast, Christian, Bible stories for kids, kindness, humility, 1 Samuel, Shiloh Creek, bedtime, ages 4-12, character

item.title: The Dark That Wasn't Empty
item.itunes:title: The Dark That Wasn't Empty
item.description: Bongo has never been scared of anything — until he's alone in the dark at the Hollow Log, fetching lantern-moss for the evening sing. When he says 'I'm scared' out loud for the first time, he discovers the dark was never empty: it's full of crickets, fireflies, his friend Professor Hoot, and God right there with him. A gentle story that you're never alone in the dark. Bible verse: Isaiah 41:10.
item.itunes:summary: Bongo has never been scared of anything — until he's alone in the dark at the Hollow Log, fetching lantern-moss for the evening sing. When he says 'I'm scared' out loud for the first time, he discovers the dark was never empty: it's full of crickets, fireflies, his friend Professor Hoot, and God right there with him. A gentle story that you're never alone in the dark. Bible verse: Isaiah 41:10.
item.itunes:subtitle: Bongo has never been scared of anything — until he's alone in the dark at the Hollow Log, fetching lantern-moss for the evening sing. When he says 'I'm scared' out loud for the first time, he discover
item.itunes:keywords: kids podcast, Christian, Bible stories for kids, fear of the dark, courage, comfort, bedtime, Isaiah, Shiloh Creek, ages 4-12, nighttime

item.title: The Turning Day
item.itunes:title: The Turning Day
item.description: Autumn comes to Shiloh Creek and the great sunflower by the rocks is done — and Zippy, who never stops, is sure the good things are ending for good. But Shelly, the quiet one who notices, kneels her down by the seeds the flower is dropping: a turning isn't an ending, it's a keeping. A gentle story about why you don't have to be afraid when things change — because the One who set the seasons turning never changes. Anchored in Malachi 3:6, 'For I am the LORD, I change not.' Ages 4-12.
item.itunes:summary: Autumn comes to Shiloh Creek and the great sunflower by the rocks is done — and Zippy, who never stops, is sure the good things are ending for good. But Shelly, the quiet one who notices, kneels her down by the seeds the flower is dropping: a turning isn't an ending, it's a keeping. A gentle story about why you don't have to be afraid when things change — because the One who set the seasons turning never changes. Anchored in Malachi 3:6, 'For I am the LORD, I change not.' Ages 4-12.
item.itunes:subtitle: Autumn comes to Shiloh Creek and the great sunflower by the rocks is done — and Zippy, who never stops, is sure the good things are ending for good. But Shelly, the quiet one who notices, kneels her d
item.itunes:keywords: kids podcast, Bible stories for kids, change, seasons, autumn, gratitude, faith, family-friendly, Malachi, God never changes, Christian kids, Shiloh Creek

item.title: The Small Good Things
item.itunes:title: The Small Good Things
item.description: The whole creek is racing toward one big morning — but Quilla, the gentle chinchilla, keeps stopping to notice the small good things everyone rushes past: a dewdrop holding the whole sky, a pebble worn smooth, the first frost-star. When the big morning lands smaller than the hype, Quilla shows Bongo the day was full of gifts all along — and every one is worth a thank-you, all the way up to God who gives them. A warm story about gratitude and noticing, anchored in Ephesians 5:20, 'Giving thanks always for all things unto God.' Ages 4-12.
item.itunes:summary: The whole creek is racing toward one big morning — but Quilla, the gentle chinchilla, keeps stopping to notice the small good things everyone rushes past: a dewdrop holding the whole sky, a pebble worn smooth, the first frost-star. When the big morning lands smaller than the hype, Quilla shows Bongo the day was full of gifts all along — and every one is worth a thank-you, all the way up to God who gives them. A warm story about gratitude and noticing, anchored in Ephesians 5:20, 'Giving thanks always for all things unto God.' Ages 4-12.
item.itunes:subtitle: The whole creek is racing toward one big morning — but Quilla, the gentle chinchilla, keeps stopping to notice the small good things everyone rushes past: a dewdrop holding the whole sky, a pebble wor
item.itunes:keywords: kids podcast, Bible stories for kids, gratitude, thankfulness, noticing, small things, faith, family-friendly, Ephesians, Christian kids, Shiloh Creek

item.title: The Laugh That Traveled
item.itunes:title: The Laugh That Traveled
item.description: It's a special one. Jigstar visits the Creek with news from far away: his friends Vera and Tega love hearing his stories about the animals — and one gloomy day, a silly Mudpuppy bit made Vera laugh and turned her day bright. The three frogs, who always thought they were just for laughs, discover that making someone smile is a kindness that travels further than they'll ever see. A warm story about how love reaches across any distance, anchored in 1 John 4:7, 'Let us love one another: for love is of God.' With a special hello to Vera and Tega. Ages 4-12.
item.itunes:summary: It's a special one. Jigstar visits the Creek with news from far away: his friends Vera and Tega love hearing his stories about the animals — and one gloomy day, a silly Mudpuppy bit made Vera laugh and turned her day bright. The three frogs, who always thought they were just for laughs, discover that making someone smile is a kindness that travels further than they'll ever see. A warm story about how love reaches across any distance, anchored in 1 John 4:7, 'Let us love one another: for love is of God.' With a special hello to Vera and Tega. Ages 4-12.
item.itunes:subtitle: It's a special one. Jigstar visits the Creek with news from far away: his friends Vera and Tega love hearing his stories about the animals — and one gloomy day, a silly Mudpuppy bit made Vera laugh an
item.itunes:keywords: kids podcast, Bible stories for kids, kindness, love, encouragement, friendship, Mudpuppies, faith, family-friendly, 1 John, Christian kids, Shiloh Creek
